content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most AI-generated songs disappear the moment they’re created. One play, maybe two, then forgotten. Not because Suno AI or Udio can’t produce good music but because most prompts are written without a plan. A vague mood, a genre name, a few adjectives, and hope.
I spent the last few months studying hundreds of AI-generated tracks that actually performed well on TikTok, Reels, and YouTube Shorts, tracks that got reused and remixed instead of disappearing into a folder. I wasn’t looking for the “best” songs. I was looking for what they had in common.
Seven patterns kept showing up, prompt after prompt, across genres and platforms.

Pattern 1 — Emotional Clarity Comes Before Genre
The clearest pattern across high-performing tracks: the prompt defined a feeling before a genre. Genre only describes instrumentation. It doesn’t tell the model what the listener should feel.
Weak: “Trap beat, dark, hard-hitting.” Strong: “A track that feels like walking home alone at 2am, replaying a conversation, dark trap production, more tension than aggression.”
Same genre, very different result. Write the emotional sentence first, genre second.
Pattern 2 — Strong Musical Identity Comes From Genre + Era + Instrumentation
“Synthpop” is vague — 1983 and 2023 synthpop barely resemble each other. Adding an era and naming the instrumentation narrows the target dramatically.
Specific: “Mid-80s synthpop — analog synth bass, gated reverb drums, bright arpeggiated lead.”
This combination showed up in nearly every track with a recognizable, non-generic identity.
Pattern 3 — Simplicity Beats Complexity
Prompts with ten-plus competing descriptors tend to produce muddier results than prompts with three or four clear ones. The model reconciles conflicting instructions by smoothing toward something generic.
The best-performing prompts rarely ran longer than two or three sentences: one emotion, one genre-era pairing, one or two structural details. Nothing more.
Pattern 4 — Hooks Matter More Than Verses
Viral tracks win or lose in the first few seconds. Short-form platforms decide attention almost instantly, so a slow build is a liability. Tracks that performed well front-loaded their identity rather than making listeners wait for it.
Worth stating explicitly in the prompt:

• “Immediate hook in the first two seconds, no slow intro”

• “Opens directly on the chorus melody”
Pattern 5 — Consistent Style Outperforms Genre-Blending
Prompts asking for a mid-song genre switch, lo-fi into trap, cinematic into EDM, usually produced tracks that sounded stitched together rather than intentional. Movement and intensity within one consistent style held together far better than switching styles entirely.
Pattern 6 — Iteration Beats Randomness
Mass-generating dozens of tracks and hoping one lands is far less reliable than generating one, identifying the single biggest issue, adjusting that one variable, and regenerating. Each iteration builds on specific information instead of starting from zero.
Pattern 7 — Building a Personal Formula Library
Consistently productive creators don’t let a working prompt disappear into their generation history. They save it, label it, and reuse the structure, swapping the emotion or reference while keeping what worked. Over time this becomes a personal system instead of a blank page every time.

The Real Takeaway
None of these seven patterns are secret techniques. They require treating prompt writing as a discipline rather than a guess — emotion before genre, simplicity over complexity, hooks up front, consistency within style, iteration over randomness, and a system for keeping what works.
That’s the real difference between an AI track that gets skipped in two seconds and one that ends up on someone else’s video three weeks later.

A Resource for Going Further
After going through hundreds of these prompts, I did what Pattern 7 describes — wrote down the formulas that consistently worked and organized them by mood, genre, and platform.
That became 100 Viral Music Formulas: Prompt Guide — ready-to-use prompt formulas and reusable frameworks built around the patterns above, meant to speed up experimentation, not replace it.
If that’s useful for your workflow: 100 Viral Music Formulas: Prompt Guide
Either way, the seven patterns hold regardless of which tool or platform you use. That part isn’t for sale — it’s just what the data showed.
